ico_gdpr.name: UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018
ico_gdpr.summary: The UK GDPR applies to any AI system that processes personal data. Core obligations include establishing a lawful basis for processing, data minimisation, purpose limitation, and transparency. If your system makes solely automated decisions with legal or similarly significant effects on individuals, Article 22 applies — giving individuals the right to request human review, an explanation of the decision, and to contest the outcome. Biometric and health data are special category data under Article 9 and require explicit consent or another Schedule 1 condition.

citations.description: Principles of processing: lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimisation, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity and confidentiality
Processing of special categories of personal data (health, biometric, genetic)
Right not to be subject to solely automated decision-making, including profiling, which produces legal or similarly significant effects

citations.document: ICO Guidance — AI and Data Protection
citations.description: Explaining AI decisions: meaningful information requirements

fca.name: FCA Consumer Duty and AI in Financial Services
fca.summary: FCA Consumer Duty (PS22/9) requires firms to deliver good outcomes across four areas: products and services, price and value, consumer understanding, and consumer support. AI systems used in financial services must be explainable and not cause foreseeable harm. The FCA's Discussion Paper DP5/22 on AI and Machine Learning sets out model risk management expectations, including documentation, validation, and ongoing monitoring of algorithmic models. Credit decisioning algorithms face additional scrutiny under Consumer Duty and FCA's fairness guidance.

citations.description: The cross-cutting rules: act in good faith, avoid foreseeable harm, enable consumers to pursue financial objectives
The four outcome rules: products and services, price and value, consumer understanding, consumer support

citations.document: FCA DP5/22 — Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning
citations.description: Model risk management: governance, validation, monitoring and ongoing oversight of AI models
A firm must pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly

cma.name: CMA Consumer Protection
cma.summary: The CMA has identified AI in consumer-facing products as a priority area. Key concerns include personalised pricing without adequate disclosure, AI-generated content that misleads consumers about the nature or origin of recommendations, and drip pricing tactics. The Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 strengthens the CMA's enforcement powers. Any consumer-facing AI that influences purchasing decisions, pricing, or product recommendations must not engage in unfair commercial practices.

citations.document: CMA — AI Foundation Models: Initial Review
citations.description: Consumer protection principles for AI-generated recommendations and content

citations.document: Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024
citations.description: Prohibition on unfair commercial practices, including misleading actions and omissions

citations.document: Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading Regulations 2008
citations.description: Misleading actions: the prohibition on false or misleading commercial communications

mhra.name: MHRA — Software as a Medical Device
mhra.summary: If your AI system is intended to diagnose, monitor, treat, or predict health conditions in individual patients, it may qualify as Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) under the Medical Devices Regulations 2002. This is one of the most heavily regulated categories for AI. Requirements include pre-market conformity assessment, technical documentation, a Unique Device Identifier, post-market surveillance, and clinical evaluation. Placing an unregistered medical device on the UK market is a criminal offence.

citations.description: Definition of medical device and software meeting that definition

citations.document: MHRA — Software and AI as a Medical Device: Guidance
citations.description: Qualification criteria: when software meets the definition of a medical device
Clinical risk management standard for health software

citations.document: MHRA — Guidance on Medical Device Registration
citations.description: Registration requirements with the MHRA before placing on the UK market

ehrc.name: Equality Act 2010 — Algorithmic Bias
ehrc.summary: The Equality Act 2010 prohibits direct and indirect discrimination across nine protected characteristics: age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Indirect discrimination can occur even without discriminatory intent — if an AI system applies a criterion or practice that disproportionately disadvantages a protected group, and is not a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim, it will be unlawful. Employers using AI in hiring, performance management, or pay decisions face the greatest exposure.

citations.description: Indirect discrimination: a provision, criterion or practice that puts persons sharing a protected characteristic at a particular disadvantage
The nine protected characteristics

citations.document: EHRC — Technical Guidance on AI and Equality
citations.description: Employer obligations: monitoring AI systems for discriminatory outcomes

citations.document: ICO and Alan Turing Institute — Explaining Decisions Made with AI
citations.description: Meaningful information requirements for automated decision-making

dsit.name: DSIT AI Governance Framework
dsit.summary: The UK Government's approach to AI regulation is sector-led and principles-based. DSIT's five cross-cutting AI principles — safety and security, appropriate transparency and explainability, fairness, accountability and governance, and contestability and redress — apply across all sectors and all regulators. While the principles are not yet statutory, they guide regulatory interpretation and are the basis for the voluntary AI Management Essentials (AIME) framework. Boards and senior leadership teams are expected to take ownership of AI governance.

citations.document: UK Government — A Pro-Innovation Approach to AI Regulation (2023 White Paper)
citations.description: The five cross-cutting principles and their intended application across sectors
Expected responsibilities of AI developers and deployers in the UK
Voluntary baseline governance framework for UK organisations deploying AI

eu_ai_act.name: EU AI Act
eu_ai_act.summary: The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) applies to any organisation placing AI systems on the EU market or using them to serve EU users — regardless of where the organisation is based. UK businesses with EU customers are in scope. High-risk AI systems (listed in Annex III — including employment decisions, credit scoring, biometric identification, and critical infrastructure) must meet requirements for technical documentation, human oversight, transparency, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity before deployment. General-purpose AI model obligations are already in force. Full high-risk system obligations apply from August 2026.

citations.description: Scope: applies to providers placing AI on the EU market and deployers using AI systems in the EU, regardless of where the provider is established
High-risk AI systems: employment and workers management; access to essential private and public services
Transparency obligations: high-risk systems must be sufficiently transparent to allow deployers to interpret output and use it appropriately
Human oversight: high-risk systems must allow for effective oversight by natural persons during operation
